content: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common neurodegenerative dementia (60-80% of cases), characterized by progressive cognitive decline, memory impairment, and functional loss. Pathological hallmarks: extracellular amyloid-beta plaques (from APP cleavage) and intracellular hyperphosphorylated tau neurofibrillary tangles. Amyloid cascade hypothesis posits Aβ accumulation initiates pathology, leading to tau spread, synaptic dysfunction, neuroinflammation, and neuronal death. Braak staging describes tau progression from entorhinal cortex to hippocampus to neocortex. Clinical stages: preclinical (biomarker positive, asymptomatic), mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to AD (objective impairment, preserved function), and AD dementia (functional impairment). Early-onset familial AD (<5%): APP, PSEN1, PSEN2 mutations. Late-onset sporadic AD: APOE ε4 allele is strongest genetic risk factor (dose-dependent). Atrophy patterns: medial temporal lobe, hippocampus, posterior cingulate, precuneus.
